ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat products — breaded and fried
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singapore-style deep-fried pork cutlet; A00GH (pork meat products) used as the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of pork cutlet is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one full piece as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or Western food stall.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Cutlet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06100029 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are taken from HPB at confidence 0.95. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally attributed to a delivery platform source in the input; these have been re-anchored to HPB Foodo values where available, and where HPB does not report a value, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Pork, fresh, loin, center rib chops, bone-in, separable lean and fat, cooked, pan-fried; USDA NDB 10062) is used as a secondary reference for cholesterol and saturated fat cross-validation. The HPB-reported values are retained as primary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore-style deep-fried pork cutlet. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food 'breadcrumbed and fried meat cutlet' referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The breadcrumb coating of refined white flour and egg is the dominant carbohydrate contributor (GI ~70 for white breadcrumbs); the pork meat itself contributes negligible carbohydrate. A blended GI of 65 is applied conservatively. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (65 × 26.655) / 100 = 17.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 202 mg/100 g; saturated fat 5.33 g/100 g; deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 202 mg/100 g is below the 400 mg/100 g high-concern threshold, but a 150 g serving delivers approximately 303 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid additional soy sauce or salt-based condiments, and prefer air-fried or baked preparation to reduce saturated fat intake, which independently elevates cardiovascular risk in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined-carbohydrate breadcrumb coating (GI ~65), 17.77 g carbohydrate/100 g, low dietary fibre 1.86 g/100 g, high fat from deep-frying worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined breadcrumb coating produces a medium-to-high glycaemic response (estimated GI 65) and the high fat content from deep-frying impairs postprandial insulin sensitivity; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to no more than 100 g, substitute wholemeal breadcrumbs to increase fibre, and choose air-frying or baking to reduce total fat and improve the overall glycaemic and metabolic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.33 g/100 g, trans fat 0.13 g/100 g, cholesterol 53.33 mg/100 g, deep-frying generating oxidised lipids and AGEs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 150 g serving delivers approximately 8 g saturated fat (approaching the daily limit for CVD patients), 0.2 g trans fat, and 80 mg cholesterol; deep-frying also generates oxidised lipids and advanced glycation end-products that promote endothelial dysfunction; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations and substitute with baked or air-fried pork loin with wholemeal coating to substantially reduce atherogenic lipid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; pork contains arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory cytokines; saturated fat 5.33 g/100 g at RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products that directly worsen joint inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation and substitute with baked or air-fried chicken breast or fish fillet using wholemeal breadcrumbs to reduce AGE formation, arachidonic acid load, and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork cutlet is a deep-fried pork dish popular in Singapore hawker centres and Western food stalls. It is prepared by coating a pork chop or pork loin slice in beaten egg and plain flour or breadcrumbs, then deep-frying in hot oil until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and a juicy, savoury pork interior. The breadcrumb coating absorbs a significant amount of cooking oil during frying, contributing to the relatively high fat content of 16.9 g per 100 g and energy density of 266 kcal per 100 g. A standard serving is approximately 150 g (one piece), delivering around 399 kcal, 25.4 g fat, 26.7 g carbohydrate, and 16.2 g protein. The dish is commonly served with rice, coleslaw, or baked beans in a Western-style plate meal context. Nutritionally, the primary concerns are the saturated fat content (5.33 g/100 g), trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), and the refined carbohydrate from the breadcrumb coating, which produces a medium glycaemic response (estimated GI 65). Sodium is moderate at 202 mg/100 g. The dish is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. It is not halal due to the use of pork. In Chinese, it is known as 猪排 (zhū pái); in Malay as kotlet babi; in Tamil as பன்றி கட்லெட்; and in Hindi as पोर्क कटलेट.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06100029, confidence 0.95 in input). HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values from the input were originally attributed to a delivery platform; these have been retained at face value as they are consistent with USDA SR Legacy cross-reference data for similar preparations, and are documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, reducing certainty on that specific field but not on the overall record.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific HPB Foodo pork cutlet entry is not reported in the HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. USDA SR Legacy (NDB 10062) provides partial fatty acid data for raw pork loin but not for the deep-fried breaded preparation. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy estimates ~220 mg/100 g for similar preparations but this is not confirmed for the Singapore HPB record and is therefore not populated. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; deep-fried pork with breadcrumb coating contains no meaningful vitamin C. 5. trans_fat_g 0.13 g/100 g: originally attributed to a delivery platform source in the input; retained as plausible for deep-fried pork (small residual trans fat from frying oil) and consistent with USDA data for similar preparations. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on breadcrumbed and fried meat cutlet from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singapore-style pork cutlet was found. GL per 150 g serving = (65 × 26.655) / 100 = 17.3 g. 7. The input dietary_tags included 'wholegrain' which is not appropriate for a standard deep-fried pork cutlet with plain breadcrumbs; this tag has been removed. The input occasion_tags included 'breakfast' which is atypical; meal_context has been updated to reflect more common consumption occasions. 8. Halal status is false as pork is not halal.
